user_prompt: inside `src\tests` implement me a new file with a I2C scan test, report all addresses that respond, follow the style of all the other tests, use the same mechanism for I2C that the ExtCodec module uses
agent_reply: Implemented the I2C scan test in [test_i2cscan.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/tests/test_i2cscan.cpp:1).

It scans `0x01..0x7E` using `M5.In_I2C.scanID(..., 400000)`, matching ExtCodec’s internal I2C mechanism, and reports all responding addresses over Serial plus a small display summary. I also registered it in [all_tests.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/tests/all_tests.h:32) and added the usual commented call in [all_tests.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/tests/all_tests.cpp:28).
Verification: `pio run -e thefly_sec1` passes. Plain `pio run -e thefly` still fails because that env does not define `BUILD_WITH_SECURITY_LEVEL`.

user_prompt: make the test a single run, do not use the screen, just one scan and print to serial port
right now it's just hung with no results, which is not possible as we know it's off the shelf hardware
agent_reply: Done. [test_i2cscan.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/tests/test_i2cscan.cpp:1) is now a one-shot serial-only scan:
- prints start status, `internal_i2c_enabled`, each found address as it appears, then the final address list and `scan complete`
I also kept `test_i2cscan()` active in [all_tests.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/tests/all_tests.cpp:28) since that’s how your tree currently had it selected.

I also kept `test_i2cscan()` active in [all_tests.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/tests/all_tests.cpp:28) since that’s how your tree currently had it selected.

user_prompt: it's not reporting any hits, which is impossible, I think? shouldn't I see the PMIC and touch sensor?
I connected a logic analyzer, I see a lot of traffic to device 0x38 and 0x34 during boot and then nothing, no scan
agent_reply: Yes, you should see at least `0x34` for PMIC and `0x38` for touch, and likely `0x51` for RTC depending on what is populated/enabled.

I updated [test_i2cscan.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/tests/test_i2cscan.cpp:1) so it no longer trusts the existing `M5.In_I2C.isEnabled()` state. That was the likely reason you saw boot traffic but no scan traffic: the test could bail before issuing any I2C transaction.
then scans `0x08..0x77` using `M5.In_I2C.scanID(address, 400000)`, so it is still the same M5/ExtCodec I2C mechanism, but forced onto the known internal bus pins `21/22`.
It also prints bus state before and after begin:
test_i2cscan: begin port=1 sda=21 scl=22 ok=...
Yes, you should see at least `0x34` for PMIC and `0x38` for touch, and likely `0x51` for RTC depending on what is populated/enabled.

I updated [test_i2cscan.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/tests/test_i2cscan.cpp:1) so it no longer trusts the existing `M5.In_I2C.isEnabled()` state. That was the likely reason you saw boot traffic but no scan traffic: the test could bail before issuing any I2C transaction.
then scans `0x08..0x77` using `M5.In_I2C.scanID(address, 400000)`, so it is still the same M5/ExtCodec I2C mechanism, but forced onto the known internal bus pins `21/22`.
It also prints bus state before and after begin:
test_i2cscan: begin port=1 sda=21 scl=22 ok=...

user_prompt: your logic here: `for (uint8_t address = kFirstAddress; address <= kLastAddress; ++address)`
I see that it is correct and incrementing by 1
on the logic analyzer, the final few scans I see are 0x73 0x75 0x77 and it stops
and I still see the normal traffic to 0x34 even though it never showed up on the scan, I see the scan try 0x33 and 0x35
this indicates the M5 library has got the bit shift wrong
So, not your fault, but I do need you to basically stop using In_I2C but use the underlying mechanism that is behind it, properly
Note: I need to use the underlying mechanism because before, we had problems using Wire.h, so whatever is behind Wire.h is not going to work
agent_reply: Changed the scanner to bypass `M5.In_I2C` entirely. [test_i2cscan.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/tests/test_i2cscan.cpp:1) now uses `m5gfx::i2c` directly:
- reports hits as `0x34(WR)`, `0x38(WR)`, etc., where `W`/`R` show which probe ACKed.

user_prompt: I just confirmed that the codec needs MCLK before it responds over I2C, see my modifications to your test
agent_reply: Good catch. I updated [src/tests/test_i2cscan.cpp](<d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/tests/test_i2cscan.cpp:135>) around that finding: it now enables shared I2S MCLK first, performs the single I2C scan while UART TX is borrowed by GPIO1/MCLK, then stops I2S, reconnects UART0 TX, and prints the stored scan results to serial.
I also removed the per-address serial prints during the scan, since those were happening while serial TX was physically disconnected.
Important follow-on: `ExtCodec::init()` still probes SGTL5000 before any production MCLK is guaranteed, so the scanner now proves the production init order needs the same treatment next.

commit_message: testing to see if SGTL5000 shows up on I2C

user_prompt: find me documentation or comments that mention how MCLK and UART conflict
agent_reply: > using the LEDC PWM generator is sufficient to generate MCLK ... leaves the UART0 pins unoccupied.
Serial.begin(115200, SERIAL_8N1, -1, 1); // RXD0/GPIO3 unused; TXD0/GPIO1 may be reclaimed for SGTL5000 MCLK.
The actual conflict-handling code is in [AudioManager.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:801):
That function only does work if `kSGTL5000I2sMclk == 1`, meaning SGTL5000 MCLK is on GPIO1, which is UART0 TX.
The I2S setup shows the two paths in [AudioManager.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:948):
Then [AudioManager.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:958) either starts LEDC MCLK or disconnects UART TX:
And one test prints the behavior explicitly in [src/tests/test_i2cscan.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/tests/test_i2cscan.cpp:130):
enabling shared I2S MCLK on GPIO%d; UART TX is disconnected during the scan
So the short version: the documented conflict is SGTL5000 I2S MCLK on GPIO1 versus UART0 TX on GPIO1. The LEDC path moves MCLK to GPIO14, which avoids stealing UART0 TX.
> using the LEDC PWM generator is sufficient to generate MCLK ... leaves the UART0 pins unoccupied.
Serial.begin(115200, SERIAL_8N1, -1, 1); // RXD0/GPIO3 unused; TXD0/GPIO1 may be reclaimed for SGTL5000 MCLK.

That function only does work if `kSGTL5000I2sMclk == 1`, meaning SGTL5000 MCLK is on GPIO1, which is UART0 TX.
The I2S setup shows the two paths in [AudioManager.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:948):
Then [AudioManager.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:958) either starts LEDC MCLK or disconnects UART TX:
And one test prints the behavior explicitly in [src/tests/test_i2cscan.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/tests/test_i2cscan.cpp:130):
enabling shared I2S MCLK on GPIO%d; UART TX is disconnected during the scan
So the short version: the documented conflict is SGTL5000 I2S MCLK on GPIO1 versus UART0 TX on GPIO1. The LEDC path moves MCLK to GPIO14, which avoids stealing UART0 TX.
